their part


e. s. Potts, financial secretary of
the trades council, is probably in a
better position than any other man
In the city to know exactly how labor
stands as regards Mayor Byrne Mr..
Potts is stationed at the offices of the
trades council every day, and he is
workmen who come to the hall to dis
cuss labor conditions, to keep In
touch with contractors desiring work
men, or to join the different labor
unions. Mr.. Potts said today:


nnAsoNs FOR REFLECTION.


'"rhe working men of this city
should reflect Mayor Byrne for more
reasons than one. One of the chief
reasons that impresses me as to why
he should be reelected is the fact that
he Is the only mayor we have ever
had who has always stood up for or





stood by the working people.


Every laboring man in this city
owes a duty to the mayor-and that
duty is to vote for him.


Furthermore, organized labor is
going to vote for Mayor Byrne this
year. I am at the hall all day long.
and l am also there at the night meet
ings. I have only heard one or two
men who opposed him. Even these
could find no fault with his record.
and the only excuse they could give
for not supporting him was that they
were bound up in one or the other
of the old parties.


Accordingly, l can honestly say
that organized labor is going to sup
port DR.. Byrne by an overwhelming
majority, and. for once, organized la
Dor Is going to stand for the man who
they sincerely believe will serve the
best interests of themselves and the
city at large"
